design_philosophy.core_loop: Tournament progression through best-of-three matches against CPU opponents, culminating in final boss confrontation with Geese Howard
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Timing and execution of special move inputs combined with strategic positioning across two-lane battle stages
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Avenge adoptive father's murder by mastering martial arts and defeating the crime boss who killed him; become King of Fighters champion
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasis on special move timing over pure execution; two-lane stage mechanic adds spatial strategy layer unusual for fighting games; narrative-driven tournament structure with story-driven character motivations rather than arcade opportunism

difficulty.lives_system: Best two-out-of-three match format; single loss in a match can be recovered from
difficulty.checkpointing: Single-player mode has progressive tournament brackets; bonus rounds (arm-wrestling mini-games) appear after every other match
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Two-lane row-switching mechanic allows players to control spatial positioning for strategic advantage; optional positioning rather than mandatory

progression.unlock_mechanism: Tournament bracket progression; player must defeat sequential opponents to advance toward final boss Geese Howard; Billy Kane acts as penultimate obstacle
replayability_hooks: Three selectable playable characters (Terry Bogard, Andy Bogard, Joe Higashi) with distinct martial arts styles
Two-player versus mode allows testing against human opponents
Two-lane positioning mechanic enables variable match strategies
Bonus round arm-wrestling mini-games for score optimization

world_building_techniques: Character backstory established in instruction materials: orphans adopted by martial arts master Jeff Bogard
Revenge motivation drives entire narrative: Geese Howard's murder of Jeff establishes clear antagonist
Geographic setting (South Town) establishes criminal underworld context for tournament
Character differentiation through martial arts training lineage (Hakkyokuseiken, Shiranui-ryū Ninjutsu, Muay Thai)
Tournament framing device (King of Fighters) provides narrative structure for progression

boss_design.philosophy: Geese Howard designed as ideological nemesis with narrative weight beyond mechanical challenge; Billy Kane positioned as guardian before final confrontation; bosses influenced by cinematic crime drama (The Godfather) for thematic resonance
notable_bosses: Geese Howard - crime boss antagonist, final boss, personal nemesis responsible for adoptive father's death
Billy Kane - penultimate boss, Geese's enforcer, thematic barrier to revenge

faction_indicators: Bogard brothers' lineage and training philosophy (Hakkyokuseiken tradition)
Joe Higashi's Thai Muay Thai heritage and outsider status
Geese Howard's criminal organization control of South Town underworld
Training mentors (Jeff Bogard, Tung Fu Rue) as philosophical anchors
danger_signals: Geese Howard established as ruthless crime boss capable of murder
Tournament context positions martial competition as life-or-death stakes
Decade-long preparation narrative suggests dangerous confrontation inevitable
environmental_storytelling: South Town setting as crime-controlled urban space
Tournament venue as Geese's power base
Martial arts dojo/training grounds as character identity anchors
Two-lane stage design suggests urban combat environments (streets, rooftops, underworld spaces)

sources_used: wikipedia - Fatal Fury: King of Fighters
